Monday is here and we are starting the third week of Protocol Study Group. It's the last week of the intro but still soon enough to catch up and start learning with us. Join us at Office Hours at 3PM UTC

Pectra Testnet Update 

On Monday, Pectra activated on Holesky. An EL bug caused chain splits, resulting in a minority chain being valid, degrading the network health.

Holesky validators must take action ASAP, some Sepolia users must upgrade their client. All info below

Where can I follow the incident? 

Over the past couple days, I've been keeping a post-mortem updated here: https://github.com/ethereum/pm/blob/master/Pectra/holesky-postmortem.md#holesky-coordinated-slashings…

Any changes to Pectra's deployment on testnets or mainnet will be announced at

It's been a wild week! There will be a lots takeaways from this incident, but for now the focus remains on getting Holesky back to stability. We can debrief later.

Thank you to everyone who has stepped up, and stayed up!, to help fix things  

See you tomorrow at 15 UTC

To add to this, we're not doing this because this is something we can replicate on mainnet as disaster mitigation. At this point, everything we're doing for Holesky is *for science.* We haven't been able to test a massive slashing at this scale nor revive a chain from multiple

If you are running a Holesky node with one of the bugged ELs we need you to update your EL to a fixed one right now. We need your validators to start proposing blocks and rely on their slashing protection to avoid being slashed. We need to get to about 75% to have a chance

RIG is the most exciting team at the core of EF research, and is hiring. If you are an exceptional person with a background in somewhere in the vicinity of economics or computer science & love solving the hardest interdisciplinary problems with the smartest people, go apply.
Come help the EF continue building the Ethereum Ivory Tower

Jokes aside, RIG is an incredible team where you'll get to work with some brilliant colleagues, thinking through possible solutions to some of the hardest problems in blockchains, you should apply 

Here's more on their work: https://rig.ethereum.org
reminder that proof-of-stake validation (along w/ the rewards "yield") is _NOT_ risk-free
If you want to help and are deeply technicaly, then consider helping us make the Mainnet fork more secure by taking part in the Pectra bug hunting contest! (13/13)

The bug presents a great testing opportunity though! We now have a non-finalizing testnet, with a unique network and in need of recovery. This was basically part of the testing on the non-finality devnets in Nov: https://x.com/parithosh_j/status/1861915497563886042…

We now get to validate our results!(10/13)

What are the complications?

An invalid chain was justified (not finalized!),due to the majority client having the bug. The nw would need to leak or slash the clients that attested to the invalid chain.Our aim is to get the chain to finalize for now, tune in to ACD for more (9/x)

How does Holesky proceed?

We will discuss this on ACD, for now:
- Resync both EL and CL with updated client releases
- If you have a validator slashing db, delete it

We want the network to finalize from the chaos and that needs your help! (8/13)

Will this affect mainnet?

Nope, the bug was scoped to config issues on Holesky. There are learnings we can take forward, but it isn't a bug with a wide surface area. We may take some time to manually verify some things, but the problem seems fully explained. (7/13)

Could we have caught this bug on a devnet? Shadowfork? 

Nope, this is a client config issue. The bug was scoped to Holesky as a named testnet. Shadowforks and devnets are custom networks, they trigger different config loading code paths.  (6/13)

ELI5 why we cannot "rollback" Ethereum?

After yesterday's Bybit hack, crypto commentators are again asking why Ethereum cannot "rollback" the chain to reverse the hack. 

While experienced ecosystem actors near-unanimously agree that this is infeasible, it's worth breaking down

Besu is the only mainnet Ethereum client which already has parallel transaction processing. We literally shipped this on Ethereum before Monad even had a testnet. If MonadDB helps us increase performance we will of course adopt it.

Having said that taking cheap shots is of

and in case anyone forgot, this is what "core devs" means... hundreds of developers from >10 different organizations and companies (plus independent contributors)

then they have to politely ask tens of thousands of node operators to update their validator software
Speaking from experience, you cannot mandate the core devs into doing things lol

There likely is, and we should do better at articulating which cases should lead to what outcome, but this is qualitatively different than interfering with valid uses of the protocol, even when reprehensible.

My MVP proposal is that such an EIP should define what social slashing actually means, incl. perhaps different degrees (e.g soft fork where we leak validators, hard fork where we specifically slash them, hard fork where you set their balances to 0, etc.), and what leads to it.

We can't perfectly anticipate the situations where this will come up, but I think there is value in having rough consensus around a rough set of offences and penalties, even if it means we end up debating whether something broke the spirit but not letter of the "law".
Social slashing makes sense for cases that are effectively "meta" attacks onj the protocol. For example, if a subset of validators constantly chose to reorg out valid blocks from the chain.

This is a completely different thing than someone using the protocol in a valid way, but for nefarious purposes. 

To take yesterday's example: it's not the "protocol action" (sending ETH) that was "wrong", it was the destination ETH address.

Important point. Censorship resistance on Ethereum currently ultimately relies on a minority of altruistic small stakers.

FOCIL will remove that reliance, and make the chain censorship resistant even with purely economically rational block proposers.
